steps.prompt: Two chords crossing like an X

graphic.name: x-cross
graphic.caption: Two chords can cross inside a circle, making an X — like a railroad diamond. The angle at the crossing opens onto two arcs.

steps.body:
A chord is a straight line joining two points on a circle. Two chords can cross inside it at a point P.
The [angle at P](magenta) opens onto two arcs: the [arc AC](teal) tucked inside it, and the [arc BD](orange) inside its mirror-image twin straight across the X.
The arc an angle opens onto is called its 'intercepted arc'.

steps.prompt: Drag C and D. Compare the angle to the half-sum.
steps.subtitle: The arcs change as you drag, but keep comparing the [angle at P](magenta) with ½ ([arc AC](teal) + [arc BD](orange)).

steps.prompt: Which arcs does the angle open onto?
steps.subtitle: Chords AB and CD cross at P. Select the TWO arcs that the [angle at P](magenta) intercepts (opens onto).

options.label: [Arc BD](orange) — inside the twin angle across the X
Arc AB — lying along one of the chords
Arc CD — lying along the other chord

feedback.correct: Yes — the angle at P opens onto [arc AC](teal) on one side and [arc BD](orange) straight across. Those are its two intercepted arcs.
feedback.incorrect: An intercepted arc is the one the angle OPENS ONTO — not the ones running along the chords. It opens onto arc AC and, across the X, arc BD.

steps.doneNote: Those two — arc AC and arc BD — are the arcs this angle will add together.

steps.prompt: Inside the circle: half the SUM
steps.body:
When two chords cross INSIDE a circle, the [angle at P](magenta) is half the SUM of the two arcs it intercepts:
[angle at P](magenta) = ½ ([arc AC](teal) + [arc BD](orange)).
Here ½ (60° + 100°) = 80°. Inside the circle, you ADD the two arcs.

gates.prompt: To bring inscribed angles in, which extra chord should we draw?
gates.hint: We want a triangle whose exterior angle at P is ∠APC.

gates.prompt: ∠DAB is an inscribed angle. It is half of which arc?
gates.hint: An inscribed angle is half the arc it opens onto — here, BD.

gates.prompt: ∠ADC is an inscribed angle. It is half of which arc?
∠APC is the exterior angle of triangle APD, so it equals…

choices.label: ½ arc BD + ½ arc AC = ½ (arc AC + arc BD).

gates.hint: An exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles.

stages: Two chords AB and CD cross at P. We will measure the angle ∠APC.
Draw the chord AD. Now ∠APC is the exterior angle of triangle APD at P.
∠DAB sits at A and opens onto arc BD, so as an inscribed angle it is ½ of arc BD.
∠ADC sits at D and opens onto arc AC, so as an inscribed angle it is ½ of arc AC.
An exterior angle equals the sum of the two remote interior angles: ∠APC = ½·arc BD + ½·arc AC = ½ (arc AC + arc BD).

steps.prompt: Add the arcs, or subtract them?
steps.subtitle: When the crossing is INSIDE the circle, you ADD the two arcs. When it's OUTSIDE, you SUBTRACT. Sort each case.

buckets.label: Inside → ADD the arcs (½ of the sum)
Outside → SUBTRACT the arcs (½ of the difference)

items.label: Two chords cross inside the circle
The vertex P is inside the circle
An X formed where two chords meet in the middle
Two secants meet at a point outside the circle
The vertex P is outside the circle

steps.doneNote: Inside the circle you ADD the arcs (½ of the sum); outside you SUBTRACT them. The outside case is the whole next lesson.

steps.subtitle: Two chords cross inside a circle, cutting off arcs of 50° and 80°. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: The two chords cross inside the circle at P.
The intercepted arcs are 50 and 80 degrees.
The vertex is inside, so the angle = 1/2 (80 - 50) = 15 degrees.
So the angle at P = 15 degrees.

steps.explanation: Inside the circle you ADD the arcs, not subtract. The [angle at P](magenta) = ½ ([arc AC](teal) + [arc BD](orange)) = ½ (50° + 80°) = 65°. Half the DIFFERENCE is the rule for a vertex OUTSIDE the circle.
steps.hint: Is the crossing inside or outside the circle? Inside means ADD the two arcs.
steps.doneNote: Right — inside the circle, the arcs ADD.

problems.prompt: Two chords cross inside a circle, cutting off arcs of 70° and 90°. Find the angle where they meet.

feedback.incorrect: Inside the circle, the angle is half the SUM of the two intercepted arcs.

problems.prompt: Two chords cross inside a circle, cutting off arcs of 40° and 100°. Find the angle where they meet.

feedback.incorrect: Inside the circle, the angle is half the SUM of the two intercepted arcs.

problems.prompt: Two chords meet at 80° inside a circle. One intercepted arc is 70°. Find the other intercepted arc.

feedback.correct: Correct. The two arcs add to 2 × 80° = 160°, so the other is 160° − 70° = 90°.
feedback.incorrect: The angle is half the sum of the arcs, so the arcs add to twice the angle.

problems.strongHint: Twice the angle is the total of both arcs: 2 × 80° − 70°.

steps.subtitle: Two chords cross inside a circle, cutting off arcs of 88° and 96°. Find the angle where they meet.

feedback.incorrect: Add the two arcs and take half.
